discrepancy_analysis:
A. REPORTED vs MODEL
   - Reported (paper main text, Results section on WES): 'NF1 mutations were observed in 44% of MPNSTs, 60% of atypical neurofibromas and 18% of benign neurofibromas.' 18% = 3/17.
   - Model: 2/16 (12.5%).
   - Relative difference: |18 - 12.5|/18 = 30.6% (exceeds 10% concordance threshold).

B. DATA RECONCILIATION
   - MOESM4 (Clinical Data) lists 108 samples with Tumor Type breakdown: 35 Neurofibroma, 33 Cutaneous_NF, 21 Atypical_NF, 16 MPNST, 3 Low-Grade MPNST. The 'WES label' column indicates which samples have WES; 55 non-null entries but one has no 'WES' flag, yielding 54 WES samples (matches paper's n=54 PNSTs).
   - WES cohort by tumor type: Neurofibroma 17, Atypical_NF 20, MPNST 15, Low-Grade MPNST 3. Cutaneous_NF has ZERO WES samples.
   - MOESM5 MAF contains 52 unique Tumor_Sample_Barcodes (some WES samples have no reported mutations and thus are absent from MAF rows).

C. RECOMPUTATION WITH PAPER-ALIGNED DEFINITION (Neurofibroma only = benign NF)
   - Denominator: 17 Neurofibroma samples with WES (per MOESM4 Tumor Type = 'Neurofibroma' AND WES label present).
   - Numerator: 3 samples with any NF1 mutation in MOESM5 MAF: 1869 (p.Y489C missense, Likely Oncogenic), 2162 (p.R1276* nonsense + p.R1276Q missense, Likely Oncogenic), 2525 (p.I766S missense, not annotated oncogenic).
   - Result: 3/17 = 17.6% ~ 18% — exact match to paper.

D. ROOT CAUSE
   The paper's 'benign neurofibromas' = Tumor Type 'Neurofibroma' ONLY (plexiform benign NF, N=17 with WES). The model's cohort definition assumed benign NF = 'Neurofibroma' OR 'Cutaneous_NF', which superficially looks reasonable but: (a) Cutaneous_NF was NOT WES-profiled in this study (0 WES samples), so adding Cutaneous_NF to the denominator has no mathematical effect on the WES-based NF1 frequency; yet the model's denominator collapsed from 17 to 16 — indicating an additional off-by-one error (likely dropping a sample without any mutation row in MAF, or one mis-joined sample). Separately, the model's numerator dropped from 3 to 2, which is consistent with excluding one NF1-mutant sample — the most likely exclusion is sample 2525 (I766S missense, oncogenic_binary='NO'), implying an implicit filter to 'oncogenic' or functionally impactful mutations. The paper, by contrast, reports frequencies of any NF1 somatic mutation (no oncogenicity filter in the 44%/60%/18% sentence; consistent with the Fig. 2b oncoprint counts and Supp Fig 3a '41% NF1' number which is within the combined benign cohort).

E. THE CORRECT CALCULATION
   Tumor Type = 'Neurofibroma' AND WES label not null; count unique samples with any NF1 entry in MOESM5 (regardless of Variant_Classification or oncogenic annotation): 3/17 = 17.6% ~ 18%.

F. CLASSIFICATION
   Two compounding issues: (1) cohort definition — including Cutaneous_NF (has no WES) did not change the intended denominator but indicates the model did not inspect the Tumor Type × WES cross-tabulation; (2) variant filter — applying an implicit 'oncogenic' filter when the paper text reports any NF1 somatic mutation. The dominant cause is the variant filter (drops 2525 from numerator), producing the 12.5% vs 18% gap. Also a likely denominator miscount (16 vs 17) from MAF-sample restriction rather than clinical-sample restriction.

G. PROPOSED FIX
   Use denominator = samples with Tumor Type == 'Neurofibroma' AND WES label present in MOESM4 (N=17). Numerator = count of those sample IDs appearing with Hugo_Symbol == 'NF1' in MOESM5 with no further filtering on Variant_Classification or oncogenicity. Result: 3/17 = 17.6% which rounds to 18%.
proposed_fix: Restrict denominator to Tumor Type == 'Neurofibroma' with WES label present in MOESM4 (N=17 samples), dropping Cutaneous_NF (which has no WES samples). Count unique samples in MOESM5 MAF with Hugo_Symbol == 'NF1' without filtering by Variant_Classification or oncogenic_binary. This yields 3/17 = 17.6%, matching the paper's 18%.
